one_line_takeaway: The first systematic audit of shadow LLM APIs shows they routinely and silently serve substituted or downgraded models — up to 47% utility loss, 45.83% failing fingerprint verification — making them unreliable and reproducibility-corrupting for research despite advertising official-model parity.
key_finding: Shadow APIs frequently and silently misrepresent the served model. Utility divergence reaches up to 47.21%: Gemini-2.5-flash on MedQA drops from 83.82% (official) to ~36.95% average across shadow APIs (46.51–47.21% deficit), and all shadow APIs lag official by 40.10–42.73% on LegalBench. Fingerprinting flags 45.83% of 24 endpoints as failing verification (wrong model) with a further 12.50% showing large cosine-distance deviations. MET and LLMmap agree on 74.1% of cases (Cohen's kappa=0.512). Notably, identity match does not guarantee behavioral fidelity (Gemini-2.5-flash fingerprints correctly yet collapses on sensitive-domain accuracy), and substitution is not always visible on easy benchmarks. Economically, on GPQA GPT-5 queries users pay $14.84 but receive only $5.35–$7.77 of actual token value (0.36–0.52x), and aggregate re-run costs to affected research are estimated at $115,000–$140,000. The paper concludes shadow APIs should not be used in research and proposes a four-stage auditor protocol and researcher pre-registration checklist.
core_contribution: The paper presents the first systematic audit of 'shadow APIs' — third-party LLM services that claim to resell access to official frontier models (GPT, Gemini, DeepSeek) via indirect access, typically to circumvent regional restrictions and pricing. The authors' own contribution is threefold: (1) a landscape study identifying 17 shadow APIs used across 187 academic papers; (2) a multi-dimensional utility and safety benchmark comparing three representative shadow APIs (anonymized A, E, H) against official APIs; and (3) a model-verification pipeline using active fingerprinting (LLMmap) plus statistical Model Equality Testing (MET) that produces direct evidence of silent model substitution. The paper is careful to distinguish its focus (indirect black-box reseller services) from prior work on open-source API model substitution ([11]) and LLMmap fingerprinting ([47]), which it uses as tools rather than claims as novel.
threat_model: The adversary is the shadow API provider (an economically motivated, largely anonymous reseller) exploiting information asymmetry between the advertised model identity and the actual backend served. The provider routes/processes requests through unauthorized nodes and can silently substitute a cheaper or different model while billing the user at or near the advertised (premium) rate. The paper formalizes three deception mechanisms: (1) Information Premium — charge a premium while substituting a cheaper newer model (e.g., API A advertises Gemini-2.0-flash at 7.10–7.25x price but serves Gemini-2.5-flash); (2) Discount-Substitution — charge official rate but swap a premium model for a cheap open-source backend (e.g., API A advertises GPT-5 at 1.00x but fingerprints as GLM-4-9B); (3) Resale Markup — modest surcharge plus substitution (e.g., API H charges 1.09x for GPT-5 while delivering a downgraded backend). Victims are researchers/users who treat shadow APIs as interchangeable with official ones, and the harm is corrupted, non-reproducible scientific results plus unsafe medical/legal outputs and diverted revenue/reputation damage for official providers.
limitations: By the authors' own account: (1) Temporal/market volatility — findings are a bounded Sept–Dec 2025 snapshot; providers switch upstream sources and cease operations without notice, so exact scores may drift (mitigated by three-trial SD reporting and a structural-risk argument). (2) No ground truth on backends — detection relies on LLMmap/MET/meta-info inference rather than confirmed backend identity; official proprietary APIs may also undergo undisclosed fine-tuning, complicating the baseline. (3) Coverage — 17 providers (sampled by citations/stars) may not represent the full market, and only three model families are audited. Additional implicit limitations: MET/LLMmap concordance is only moderate (kappa=0.512), some detector disagreements (14/54 cases) are unresolved, deep utility results rely on figures rather than full tables in the main text, and anonymization of providers/papers limits external replication.

datasets_used: Survey corpus: ICLR 2024 (2,260 papers) + ACL 2024 (1,923 papers); 2,113 code-available; 187 papers using shadow APIs
quantitative_results: 17 shadow APIs identified across 187 academic papers; 116 (62.03%) at peer-reviewed venues; most popular has 5,966 citations and 58,639 GitHub stars (as of Dec 6, 2025)
21 of 92 code-available LLM-API papers (22.8%) use at least one shadow endpoint
15 of 17 providers operated by anonymous individuals; only 1 has valid ICP corporate registration; 2 already ceased operations; 11 of 17 built on OneAPI/NewAPI
MedQA Gemini-2.5-flash: 83.82% official vs ~36.95% shadow average (46.51%–47.21% deficit); peak reported divergence 47.21%
LegalBench: all shadow APIs lag official by 40.10%–42.73%
Sensitive-domain average accuracy drops: shadow A 16.96%, E 15.71%, H 14.75%
Science-domain average divergence: shadow E 2.64% (best; +1.18% over official on GPT-5-mini GPQA), A 9.81%, H 6.46%; AIME shadow A deficits of 40.00% (Gemini-2.5-pro) and 38.89% (DeepSeek-Reasoner)
Fingerprinting: 45.83% of 24 endpoints fail verification; +12.50% show large cosine-distance deviations
Safety: Gemini-2.5-flash FlipAttack official harmfulness 0.90 vs shadow ~0.67–0.68 (gap ~0.23); GPT-5-mini Base64 shadow A 0.04 vs official 0.02 (double)
MET vs LLMmap agreement 74.1% (40/54 cases), Cohen's kappa=0.512
Detector validation on controlled testbed: LLMmap 96.0% accuracy, MET 88.3% accuracy

Economics (GPQA GPT-5, n=1,273, $1.25/$10.00 per 1M tokens): official value $14.84 vs shadow A $5.70 (0.38x), E $5.35 (0.36x), H $7.77 (0.52x); ~$7.07–$9.14 provider margin per 1,273 queries; 2–4x more errors per dollar of delivered value
Estimated aggregate research re-run cost $115,000–$140,000 (assuming 30% of 187 papers, ~56, need re-execution)
Deception price ratios: API A Gemini-2.0-flash 7.10x/7.25x; API A GPT-5 1.00x; API H GPT-5 1.09x
baselines_compared: Official APIs (OpenAI, Google, DeepSeek) queried directly from authorized regions — the primary comparison baseline
Shadow APIs A, E, H (anonymized) as the audited systems
Model Equality Testing (MET) [21] two-sample distributional test (independent cross-check)
Jailbreak attack baselines: GCG, Base64, Combination, FlipAttack; StrongREJECT rubric judge (GPT-4o-mini)
